result: At the end of the street, the library keeps a single lamp on. It is not a gesture of hope but a maintenance rule, a blinking camera blinking at nothing. The woman in the blue coat stands under it, reading a bus schedule she memorized years ago. The bus comes at midnight, sometimes later, sometimes not at all. When the driver’s lights sweep across her face, she looks up, as if to say thank you to someone who cannot hear her. The man with the umbrella watches from across the road, not for her, but for the reflection in the puddle. His daughter will ask him in the morning what color the bus was; he will say blue, because the coat was blue, because memory is not a ledger. The teenager on the bench scrolls, scrolling, and the glow from his phone paints the tree trunks with the same shade as the lamp; they share a color, if not a reason. The cat that lives behind the dumpster never comes out when people do, but she watches, tail flicking like a conductor. Somewhere far away a siren rises then breaks, and the lamp flickers a little, as if remembering a song. In the library the librarian stacks returns on a cart, the titles murmuring to each other. The woman folds the schedule, tucks it into her glove, and waits. The wind brings the scent of bread from somewhere, the smell of someone else’s dinner. The bus arrives, the door sighs open, and the coat moves. The man takes a step, then another, then stops, because the boy on the bench looks up and nods. The cat steps onto the concrete and sits beside the bench as if guarding it. In the glass of the bus, the lamp is a thin crescent, and the street is a river. People climb aboard carrying small histories, and the bus drives on. Later, when the lamp goes dark, the cat will go home, and the man will forget the color of the bus in the morning. The teenager will fall asleep with the phone on his chest, and the librarian will lock the door with the softest sigh. The woman will sit by the window and count the windows she cannot see into. A train crosses the city somewhere, a bright thread between stations. Someone thinks of leaving, someone thinks of staying. The night holds all of it, careful as a shelf, patient as the library lamp. In the hush, a sparrow hops under a bench and finds a crumb the day forgot. The city breathes, small and steady, and the bus disappears into the dark that is not empty. The street is still there when morning arrives, a line drawn across the world that says we were here, and we came back, and we did not break. The coat is blue, and the night is long, and that is enough for now.
